source: Derived from marking key model answer gap analysis

improvement_patterns.example_after: primary aromas of ripe black cherry, plum, and cassis

improvement_patterns.example_before: I think this is a very good wine
improvement_patterns.example_after: Very good quality: medium(+) intensity, secondary oak complexity (vanilla, cedar), balanced structure, medium(+) finish
In cool climates, lower temperatures slow sugar accumulation and preserve malic acid, resulting in higher acidity in the finished wine

improvement_patterns.example_before: Chablis is cool. Meursault uses oak.
improvement_patterns.example_after: Chablis is cool and unoaked, producing lean mineral Chardonnay; Meursault has a slightly warmer mesoclimate and typically uses oak ageing, producing fuller, richer wines

improvement_patterns.example_before: The palate has red fruit and oak flavours
improvement_patterns.example_after: Dry; high acidity; medium tannin; medium(+) alcohol; medium(+) body; medium(+) intensity; primary red cherry, secondary vanilla and cedar; medium(+) finish

improvement_patterns.from: Tertiary aromas omitted for complex wine
improvement_patterns.example_before: Aromas of red cherry, vanilla, and toast
improvement_patterns.example_after: Primary: red cherry, raspberry. Secondary: vanilla, cedar (oak), buttery (MLF). Tertiary: leather, earth, forest floor (bottle development)
improvement_patterns.applies_to: SAT nose + quality assessment for outstanding wines
